headline: China's Desert Barrier Halts Decades of Sand Expansion
meta_description: Half a century of effort using 'straw checkerboards' has helped China's Green Great Wall reverse desertification since 2000.
summary_short: For fifty years, China has used simple, ingenious methods to stop desert expansion across its northern regions.
body:
In northern China's Kubuqi Desert, a half-century effort to reverse desertification has achieved remarkable results. The Three-North Protective Forest Program, known as the Green Great Wall, employs a deceptively simple technique: laying straw in checkerboard patterns across sand dunes.

This humble method stabilizes the sand, creates pockets where plants can take root, and gradually transforms barren expanses into vegetated land. Since 2000, the program has measurably slowed and in many places reversed the creep of desert that had threatened to swallow vast regions. Thousands of workers have carried out this painstaking work year after year.

While scientists caution that the battle against desertification continues, the visible transformation of the landscape offers proof that sustained, determined action can heal even severely degraded land. The Green Great Wall stands as a testament to patience and persistence in environmental restoration.
